title: ISSS608-VAA
text: Welcome to ISSS608 Visual Analytics and Applications. In this website, you will find my coursework prepared for this course.

title: Hands-on Excercise 1 - Layered Grammar of Graphics: ggplot Methods
text:
The code chunk below uses pacman package’s p_load() to check if tidyverse packages are installed in the computer. If they are, then they will be launched into R.

pacman::p_load(tidyverse)





exam_data <- read.csv("Exam_data.csv")

In RStudio, can view the dataset in the environment pane of the top right side after running the first two codes. There should be the data and clicking on it will reveal the data.
But it is possible to code it out as shown in the code chunk below. This will output all the rows and columns.

print(exam_data)

To just show the the top few records of the data, use this code:

head(exam_data)

          ID CLASS GENDER    RACE ENGLISH MATHS SCIENCE
1 Student321    3I   Male   Malay      21     9      15
2 Student305    3I Female   Malay      24    22      16
3 Student289    3H   Male Chinese      26    16      16
4 Student227    3F   Male Chinese      27    77      31
5 Student318    3I   Male   Malay      27    11      25
6 Student306    3I Female   Malay      31    16      16


Or if just want to see a statistical overview of the data, use this code instead:

summary(exam_data)

      ID               CLASS              GENDER              RACE          
 Length:322         Length:322         Length:322         Length:322        
 Class :character   Class :character   Class :character   Class :character  
 Mode  :character   Mode  :character   Mode  :character   Mode  :character  
                                                                            
                                                                            
                                                                            
    ENGLISH          MATHS          SCIENCE     
 Min.   :21.00   Min.   : 9.00   Min.   :15.00  
 1st Qu.:59.00   1st Qu.:58.00   1st Qu.:49.25  
 Median :70.00   Median :74.00   Median :65.00  
 Mean   :67.18   Mean   :69.33   Mean   :61.16  
 3rd Qu.:78.00   3rd Qu.:85.00   3rd Qu.:74.75  
 Max.   :96.00   Max.   :99.00   Max.   :96.00

title: Hands-on Excercise 1 - Layered Grammar of Graphics: ggplot Methods
text:
The code chunk below uses pacman package’s p_load() to check if tidyverse packages are installed in the computer. If they are, then they will be launched into R.

pacman::p_load(tidyverse)

title: Hands-on Excercise 1 - Layered Grammar of Graphics: ggplot Methods
text:
exam_data <- read.csv("Exam_data.csv")

In RStudio, can view the dataset in the environment pane of the top right side after running the first two codes. There should be the data and clicking on it will reveal the data.
But it is possible to code it out as shown in the code chunk below. This will output all the rows and columns.

title: Hands-on Excercise 1 - Layered Grammar of Graphics: ggplot Methods
text:
R Graphics VS ggplot

R Graphicsggplot2



hist(exam_data$MATHS)











ggplot(data=exam_data, aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 10, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color = 'black',
                 fill = 'grey') +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores")

title: Hands-on Excercise 1 - Layered Grammar of Graphics: ggplot Methods
text:
Bar chart: geom_bar()

ggplot(data = exam_data,
       aes(x= RACE)) +
  geom_bar()

title: Hands-on Excercise 1 - Layered Grammar of Graphics: ggplot Methods
text:
Dot plot: geom_dotplot()
Width of dot corresponds to the bin width (or max width, depending on binning algorithm), and dots are stacked, with each dot representing one observation.

ggplot(data = exam_data,
       aes(x= MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot(dotsize = 0.5)














WarningBe careful



The y scale is not very useful and in fact is misleading. As such it is better to turn off y-axis using scale_y_continous().



ggplot(data=exam_data, aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot(binwidth=2, dotsize = 0.5) +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL, breaks = NULL)














Note



The difference in the first and the second code is the additional binwidth argument. The binwidth controls the binning. The dotsize is scaling the drawing of the dots. So just having binwidth argument means binning is controlled and ggplot automatically default the dot size to 1, as shown below. Having only the dotsize argument means ggplot bins according to its default but size of the dot is controlled as shown in the first code. And having both arguments means that ggplot have to follow the binning and size of the dot specified as shown in the second code.



ggplot(data = exam_data,
       aes(x= MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot(binwidth = 2.5)

title: Hands-on Excercise 1 - Layered Grammar of Graphics: ggplot Methods
text:
Histograms: geom_histograms()
The code chunk below is an example to create a simple histogram by using values in MATHS field of exam_data

ggplot(data=exam_data, aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram()

title: Hands-on Excercise 1 - Layered Grammar of Graphics: ggplot Methods
text:
Density: geom_density()
geom_density() computes and plots kernel density estimate, which is smoothed version of the histogram. Useful alternative to the histogram for continuous data that comes from an underlying smooth distribution.

ggplot(data=exam_data, aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_density()

title: Hands-on Excercise 1 - Layered Grammar of Graphics: ggplot Methods
text:
Boxplot: geom_boxplot()
geom_boxplot() displays continous value list. It visualises five summary statistics (the median, two hinges and two whiskers), and all “outlying” points individually.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, x = GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot()








Notches are used in box plots to help visually assess whether medians of distributions differ. If the notches do not overlap, this is evidence that the medians are different.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, x = GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot(notch = TRUE)








From this, it can be seen that the median is not the same.

title: Hands-on Excercise 1 - Layered Grammar of Graphics: ggplot Methods
text:
Violin plot: geom_violin()
geom_violin() creates violin plot which is a way to compare multiple data distributions. With ordinary density curves, it is difficult to compare more than just a few distributions because the lines visually interfere with each other. With a violin plot, it’s easier to compare several distributions since they’re placed side by side.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, x = GENDER)) +
  geom_violin()

title: Hands-on Excercise 1 - Layered Grammar of Graphics: ggplot Methods
text:
Scatterplot: geom_point()

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS, y = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point()

title: Hands-on Excercise 1 - Layered Grammar of Graphics: ggplot Methods
section: Modifying geometric object by changing geom()
text:
Modifying geometric object by changing geom()
Lets use histogram as an example. Bins argument is the number of bins. Fill argument is the shade of the histogram. Color is the outline color of the bars. So, the code below means that the number of bins is 20, with light blue shade outlined with black.

ggplot(data=exam_data, aes(x= MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, color='black', fill='light blue')

title: Hands-on Excercise 1 - Layered Grammar of Graphics: ggplot Methods
section: Modifying geometric object by changing aes()
text:
Modifying geometric object by changing aes()
We could add a subgroup by using colour, fill, shape argument in aes().

ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(x = MATHS, 
           colour = GENDER)) +
  geom_density()









ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS, 
           fill = GENDER)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, color='black')









ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(x = MATHS, 
           y = ENGLISH, 
           shape = GENDER)) +
  geom_point()














Note



Scatterplot can use shape and colour. Bar and histogram can use fill. Density can use colour. Dot plot can use shape, colour and fill. Alpha can also be used in aes() which controls the opacity.

title: Hands-on Excercise 1 - Layered Grammar of Graphics: ggplot Methods
text:
geom objects can be combined
The code chunk below combine scatterplot and boxplot.

ggplot (data=exam_data,
        aes(y = MATHS, 
            x = GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  geom_point(position='jitter', size = 0.5)














Note



Jitter is used for the position so that a random noise is given to very point, which can prevent overplotting where dots might overlap.

title: Hands-on Excercise 1 - Layered Grammar of Graphics: ggplot Methods
text:
stat_summary() method
Below is a simple boxplot that is incomplete because the positions of the means are not shown.

ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(y = MATHS, x = GENDER))+
  geom_boxplot()








Now add stat_summary() function and override default geom.

ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(y = MATHS, x = GENDER))+
  geom_boxplot() +
  stat_summary(geom = 'point',
               fun = 'mean',
               colour = 'red',
               size = 4)

title: Hands-on Excercise 1 - Layered Grammar of Graphics: ggplot Methods
text:
geom() method

ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(y = MATHS, x = GENDER))+
  geom_boxplot() +
  geom_point(stat = 'summary',
             fun = 'mean',
             colour = 'red',
             size = 4)

title: Hands-on Excercise 1 - Layered Grammar of Graphics: ggplot Methods
section: Adding best fit curve on scatterplot
text:
Adding best fit curve on scatterplot
Below is a scatterplot between Maths and English grades of students.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS, y = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point()








The interpretability of this graph can be improved by adding a best fit curve. This is done using geom_smooth().

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS, y = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(size = 0.5)








The default smoothing method used is loess. But this can be overridden as shown below.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS, y = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = lm,
              linewidth = 0.5)

title: Hands-on Excercise 1 - Layered Grammar of Graphics: ggplot Methods
text:
facet_wrap() function
facet_wrap() wraps a 1d sequence of panels into 2d. This is generally a better use of screen spae than facet_grid() because most displays are roughly rectangular.
The code chunk below plots a trellis plot using facet_wrap().

ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20) +
  facet_wrap(~ CLASS)








It is also possible to do a combination as shown in the code chunk below.

ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20) +
  facet_wrap(~ CLASS + GENDER)

title: Hands-on Excercise 1 - Layered Grammar of Graphics: ggplot Methods
text:
facet_grid() function
facet_grid() forms a matrix of panels defined by row and column facetting variables. It is most useful when you have two discrete variables, and all combinations of the variables exits in the data.
The code chink below plots a trellis plot using facet_grid().

ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20) +
  facet_grid(~ GENDER)








The graph plot the trellis plot through column. It is possible to change the orientation to rows instead.

ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20) +
  facet_grid(rows = vars(GENDER))








Again it is possible to do a combination as shown in the code chunk below.

ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20) +
  facet_grid(vars(CLASS), vars(GENDER))

title: Hands-on Excercise 1 - Layered Grammar of Graphics: ggplot Methods
text:
coord_flip() function
By default, bar chart of ggplot2 is in vertical form.

ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(x = RACE)) +
  geom_bar()








The code chunk below flips the horizontal bar chart into vertical bar chart by using coord_flip().

ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(x = RACE)) +
  geom_bar() +
  coord_flip()

title: Hands-on Excercise 1 - Layered Grammar of Graphics: ggplot Methods
text:
coord_cartesian() function
The scatterplot below is slightly misleading because the y-axis and the x-axis range are not equal.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS, y = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = lm,
              linewidth = 0.5)








Lets fix both the y-axis and x-axis range from 0-100. The code chunk below does that.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS, y = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = lm,
              linewidth = 0.5) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0,100),
                  ylim = c(0,100))

title: About
text:
About this site

1 + 1

title: Hands-on Exercise 2 - Beyond ggplot2 Fundamentals
text:
For this exercise, besides tidyverse, four R packages will be used. They are:

ggrepel: provides geoms for ggplot2 to repel overlapping text labels
ggthemes: provides some extra themes, geoms, and scales for ggplot2
hrbrthemes: provides typography-centric thems and theme components for ggplot2
patchwork: for preparing composite figure created using ggplot2

The code chunk below will check if the packages needed are installed and load them into R environment.

pacman::p_load(ggrepel, patchwork, ggthemes,
               hrbrthemes, tidyverse)




The data file used is called Exam_data which consists of year end examination grades of a cohort of primary 3 students from a local school. It is in csv file format.

exam_data <- read_csv("Exam_data.csv")
summary(exam_data)

      ID               CLASS              GENDER              RACE          
 Length:322         Length:322         Length:322         Length:322        
 Class :character   Class :character   Class :character   Class :character  
 Mode  :character   Mode  :character   Mode  :character   Mode  :character  
                                                                            
                                                                            
                                                                            
    ENGLISH          MATHS          SCIENCE     
 Min.   :21.00   Min.   : 9.00   Min.   :15.00  
 1st Qu.:59.00   1st Qu.:58.00   1st Qu.:49.25  
 Median :70.00   Median :74.00   Median :65.00  
 Mean   :67.18   Mean   :69.33   Mean   :61.16  
 3rd Qu.:78.00   3rd Qu.:85.00   3rd Qu.:74.75  
 Max.   :96.00   Max.   :99.00   Max.   :96.00  


From the summary, there area total of seven attributes in the exam_data data frame. Four of them are categorical data type and the other three are continuous data type.

Categorical attributes are: ID, CLASS, GENDER and RACE
Continuous attributes are: MATHS, ENGLISH and SCIENCE

The code chunk below will check if the packages needed are installed and load them into R environment.

               hrbrthemes, tidyverse)

title: Hands-on Exercise 2 - Beyond ggplot2 Fundamentals
text:
ggtheme package
ggtheme provides ggplot2 themes that replicate the look fo plots by Edward Tufte, Stephen Few, Fivethirteight, The Economist, Stata, Excel and The Wall Street Journal among others.
The code below used The Exonomist theme.

ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color = "grey25",
                 fill = "grey90") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths score") +
  theme_economist()














Tip



It has many themes and provides some extra geoms and scales for ggplot2. Consult this site to see the various options.

title: Hands-on Exercise 2 - Beyond ggplot2 Fundamentals
text:
hrbrthemes packages
hrbrthemes package provides base theme that focuses on typograohic elements including where various lables are placed as well as the fonts that are used.

ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color = "grey25",
                 fill = "grey90") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths score") +
  theme_ipsum()








The second goal centers around productivity for a production workflow where presenting something needs to be visually clean and readable to increase reader’s engagement. Consult this site to learn more.

ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color = "grey25",
                 fill = "grey90") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths score") +
  theme_ipsum(axis_title_size = 18,
              base_size = 15,
              grid = 'Y')














TipWhat can be learnt from the code chunk above?




axis_title_size: controls font size of the axis title
base_size: controls the size of axis label
grid: controls the lines in the graph, in this case remove the x-axis grid lines

title: Hands-on Exercise 2 - Beyond ggplot2 Fundamentals
text:
patchwork methods
There are several extension function that support composite figure by combining several graphs such as grid.arrange() of gridExtra package and plot_grid() of cowplot package. This section will focus on an extension called patchwork which is specially designed for combining ggplot2 graphs into a single figure.
Patchwok package has a very simple syntax wherewe can create layouts super easily. Here’s the general syntax:

Two-column layout using the plus sign +
Subplot group ysing paranthesis ()
Two-row layout using division sign /
Plot beside each other using |

Examples:

Combine 2 ggplot graphscombine 3 ggplot graphs



p1 + p2











(p1 / p2) | p3

















Tip



Another way is to use wrap_plots() to directly plot all the graphs into one section. Use #| fig-width and #| fig-height to change the size or scale of the figure. This one uses 15 and 7 respectively.

wrap_plots(p1, p2, p3)










To learn more, refer to Plot Assembly. The site has information about adding a table or some text and how to place it to the left of the graph.

title: Hands-on Exercise 2 - Beyond ggplot2 Fundamentals
text:
Creating composite figure with tag
In order to identify subplots in text, patchwork also provides auto-tagging capabilities as shown below.

((p1 / p2) | p3) +
  plot_annotation(tag_levels = 'I')

title: Hands-on Exercise 2 - Beyond ggplot2 Fundamentals
text:
Creating figure with inser
It is also possible to insert one or more plots or graphic elements freely on top or below another plot using inset_element().

p3 + inset_element(p2, 
                   left = 0.02, 
                   bottom = 0.7, 
                   right = 0.5, 
                   top = 1,
                   align_to = 'full')














Note



align_to specifies where the element should be relative to. ‘panel’ is the default, ‘full’ is for the whole image, ‘plot’ is for the plot area of the main layer.

title: Hands-on Exercise 2 - Beyond ggplot2 Fundamentals
section: Creating composite figure using patchwork and ggtheme
text:
Creating composite figure using patchwork and ggtheme

patchwork <- ((p1 / p2) | p3) +
  plot_annotation(tag_levels = "1")
patchwork & theme_economist(dkpanel=TRUE) 














Tip



It is not possible to add both ggtheme and hrbrtheme as both as solid themes so adding both would result on the last theme added. For example if the code was pathwork & theme_economist() & theme_ipsum() the output will have the ipsum theme and no economist theme.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3 - Interactive Data Visualisation
text:
Getting Started
R packages needed:

ggiraph: making ggplot graphic interactive
plotly: plotting interactive statistical graphs
DT: provide a R interface to JavaScript library DataTables that create interactive table on html page
tidyverse: support data science, analysis and communication task including creating static graphs
patchwork: combine multiple ggplot graphs into one figure

title: Hands-on Exercise 3 - Interactive Data Visualisation
text:
R packages needed:

ggiraph: making ggplot graphic interactive
plotly: plotting interactive statistical graphs
DT: provide a R interface to JavaScript library DataTables that create interactive table on html page
tidyverse: support data science, analysis and communication task including creating static graphs
patchwork: combine multiple ggplot graphs into one figure


pacman::p_load(ggiraph, plotly, patchwork,
               DT, tidyverse)




Exam_data.csv is used for this exercise. read_csv() is used to read the csv file and import Exam_data into R.

exam_data <- read_csv("Exam_data.csv")

title: Hands-on Exercise 3 - Interactive Data Visualisation
text:
R packages needed:

ggiraph: making ggplot graphic interactive
plotly: plotting interactive statistical graphs
DT: provide a R interface to JavaScript library DataTables that create interactive table on html page
tidyverse: support data science, analysis and communication task including creating static graphs
patchwork: combine multiple ggplot graphs into one figure


pacman::p_load(ggiraph, plotly, patchwork,
               DT, tidyverse)

title: Hands-on Exercise 3 - Interactive Data Visualisation
text:
Exam_data.csv is used for this exercise. read_csv() is used to read the csv file and import Exam_data into R.

exam_data <- read_csv("Exam_data.csv")

title: Hands-on Exercise 3 - Interactive Data Visualisation
text:
Tooltip effect
The code chunk below is a typical code chunk to plot an interactive statistical graph by using ggiraph package.

p <- ggplot(data=exam_data, aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(
    aes(tooltip = ID),
    stackgroups = TRUE,
    binwidth = 1,
    method = "histodot") +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL, breaks = NULL)

girafe(
  ggobj = p,
  width_svg = 6,
  height_svg = 6*0.618
)











Note



Notice that the code chunk consists of two parts. First, an interactive version of ggplot object will be created. In this case, geom_dotplot_interactive() is used to create the basic graph. Next, girafe() of ggiraph will be used to create an interactive svg object to be displayed on an html page.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3 - Interactive Data Visualisation
text:
Displaying multiple information on tooltip
The content of the tooltip can be customized by including a list object as shown in the code chunk below.

exam_data$tooltip <- c(paste0(     
  "Name = ", exam_data$ID,         
  "\n Class = ", exam_data$CLASS)) 

p <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(
    aes(tooltip = exam_data$tooltip), 
    stackgroups = TRUE,
    binwidth = 1,
    method = "histodot") +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,               
                     breaks = NULL)
girafe(
  ggobj = p,
  width_svg = 8,
  height_svg = 8*0.618
)





The first 3 lines in the code is create a column called tooltip in Exam_data which is filled with the combined text of ID and CLASS values. Now this will act as the label for each student. Then this field is then called in line 7 tooltip = exam_data(tooltip) so that when building the chart it will use the new tooltip column instead.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3 - Interactive Data Visualisation
text:
Customizing tooltip sytle
opts_tooltip() of ggiraph is used to customize tooltip rendering by add css declaration. Refer to the code chunk below.

tooltip_css <- "background-color:white; #<<
font-style:bold; color:black;" #<<

p <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(              
    aes(tooltip = ID),                   
    stackgroups = TRUE,                  
    binwidth = 1,                        
    method = "histodot") +               
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,               
                     breaks = NULL)
girafe(                                  
  ggobj = p,                             
  width_svg = 6,                         
  height_svg = 6*0.618,
  options = list(    #<<
    opts_tooltip(    #<<
      css = tooltip_css)) #<<
)                                        





Notice that the background color of the tooltip is black adn font color is white and bold.
Refer to this article to learn more about customizing girafe objects.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3 - Interactive Data Visualisation
text:
Displaying statistics on tooltip
Code chunk below shows an advanced way to customise tooltip. Int his example, a function is used to cimpute 90% confident interval of the mean. The derived statistics are then displayed in the tooltip.

tooltip <- function(y, ymax, accuracy = .01) {
  mean <- scales::number(y, accuracy = accuracy)
  sem <- scales::number(ymax - y, accuracy = accuracy)
  paste("Mean maths score:", mean, "+/-", sem)
}

gg_point <- ggplot(data=exam_data, aes(x = RACE)) +
  stat_summary(aes(y = MATHS, 
                   tooltip=after_stat(
                     tooltip(y, ymax))),
               fun.data = "mean_se",
               geom = GeomInteractiveCol,
               fill = "light blue") +
  stat_summary(aes(y = MATHS), 
               fun.data = mean_se,
               geom = "errorbar", width = 0.2, size = 0.2)

girafe(ggobj = gg_point,
       width_svg = 8,
       height_svg = 8*0.618)

title: Hands-on Exercise 3 - Interactive Data Visualisation
text:
Styling hover effect
The code chunk below will change the css code for the highlighting effect.

p <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(
    aes(data_id = CLASS),
    stackgroups = TRUE,
    binwidth = 1,
    method = "histodot") +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,
                     breaks = NULL)
girafe(
  ggobj = p,
  width_svg = 6,
  height_svg = 6*0.618,
  options = list(
    opts_hover(css = "fill: #202020;"),
    opts_hover_inv(css = "opacity:0.2;")
  )
)











Note



The default css style is an orange fill. In this example, the css in opts_hover() would mean the data points that are hovered over will have a close to black fill. But the css style of other data points will have their opacity reduced to 0.2 as set in opts_hover_inv().

title: Hands-on Exercise 3 - Interactive Data Visualisation
text:
plot_ly() method

BasicWith visual argument



plot_ly(data = exam_data, 
             x = ~MATHS, 
             y = ~ENGLISH)








plot_ly(data = exam_data, 
        x = ~ENGLISH, 
        y = ~MATHS, 
        color = ~RACE)





If hovered over the data points, it will also say the Race.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3 - Interactive Data Visualisation
text:
ggplotly() method

p <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
            aes(x = MATHS,
                y = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point(size=1) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100))
ggplotly(p)





Notice that the only extra line in the code chunk is ggplotly()

title: Hands-on Exercise 3 - Interactive Data Visualisation
text:
Coordinate multiple views with plotly
The creation of coordinated linked plot by using plotly incolves three steps:

highlight_key() of plotly package is used as shared data
two scatterplots will be created by using ggplot functions
subplot() of plotly package is used to place them next to each other side-by-side


d <- highlight_key(exam_data)
p1 <- ggplot(data=d, 
            aes(x = MATHS,
                y = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point(size=1) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100))

p2 <- ggplot(data=d, 
            aes(x = MATHS,
                y = SCIENCE)) +
  geom_point(size=1) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100))

subplot(ggplotly(p1),
        ggplotly(p2))





In terms of interactivity, by clicking a data points of one of the scatterplot will also select the corresponding point on the other scatterplot.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3 - Interactive Data Visualisation
text:
Interactive data table
Data objects in R can be rendered as HTML tables using the JavaScript library DataTables.

DT::datatable(exam_data, class= "compact")

title: Hands-on Exercise 3 - Interactive Data Visualisation
text:
Linked brushing
It is possible to create interactive coordinated visalisations where selecting data points in one widget automatically highlights or filters the corresponding data in another widget. Brush here mean click or drag to select data points in one chart. So a linked brushing means that by dragging to select data points in one chart, the same data points are simultaneously highlighted in another chart or table.

CodeOutput plot



d <- highlight_key(exam_data) 
p <- ggplot(d, 
            aes(ENGLISH, 
                MATHS)) + 
  geom_point(size=1) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100))

gg <- highlight(ggplotly(p),        
                "plotly_selected")  

crosstalk::bscols(gg,               
                  DT::datatable(d), 
                  widths = 5)        







Note




highlight() is a function of plotly package. It sets a variety of optins for brushing multiple plots. These options are primarily designed for linking multuple ploly graphs, and may not behave as expected when linking plotly to another htmlwidget package via crosstalk. In some cases, other htmlwidgets will respect these options, such as persistent selection in leaflet.
bscols() is a helper function of crosstalk package It makes it easy to put HTML elements side by side. It can be called directly from the console but is especially designed to work in a R Markdown document. Warning: This will bring in all of Bootstrap!
